text: welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery today we're talking about one of my favorite adjuncts to modern day hernia surgery and that's the madrid modification to the transversus abdominis release this case was performed on a woman with a history of cirrhosis whom we had optimized from a child's class b to a child's class a working with our hepatology team She had a surgical history notable for strangulated umbilical hernia requiring emergency bowel resection and primary repair, along with an episode of laparoscopic lysive adhesions and a transvaginal hysterectomy.

These are her representative images from her CT scan. Wide mouth, central defect measuring nine by ten centimeters. She had a rectus width of about nine centimeters. And so we felt she was a good candidate for at least an ETEP approach with a low likelihood to perform TAR.
The surgeon performs an ETEP crossover dissection on the left side, inadvertently entering the peritoneum, causing the bowel to visibly fall away.
The surgeon performs a quick crossover technique, entering the peritoneal cavity and developing a retromuscular dissection to manage large posterior defects.

The surgeon attempts to recruit the hernia sac after entering the peritoneal cavity, revealing a large defect in the posterior sheath. Various strategies are employed to close the posterior sheath without undue tension, including recruiting approximately a quarter of the hernia sac for closure of the visceral sac.

The surgeon completes most of the retromuscular dissection, with limited visualization of the dissection into the space of Retzius. The bladder is mobilized to aid in addressing the central defect, requiring creative strategies to bridge the posterior defect and exclude the mesh.

The surgeon confirms deep paralysis and adequate retromuscular dissection. The video demonstrates the Madrid modification to the transversus abdominis release (TAR), a muscle-sparing approach that develops a preperitoneal, pre-transversalis dissection without transecting the transversus abdominis muscle.

The surgeon enters the space of Bogros around the arcuate line, developing the space laterally into the retroperitoneum. The dissection reveals a thin preperitoneal plane, with multiple holes made during the challenging dissection process.

The surgeon completes the dissection from the bottom up to the subdiaphragmatic region, incising a small segment of the transversus abdominis due to multiple holes in the peritoneum. The procedure concludes with a tension-free closure of the visceral sac, showcasing a loose posterior flap resting on the viscera.

The surgeon proceeds with anterior fascial closure using a number one permanent V-lock suture on a GS-XXI needle, plicating the diastasis and working towards defect closure. The surgical field appears oozy, possibly due to the patient's history of portal hypertension and collateralization in the abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs plication of the diastasis followed by defect closure, focusing on re-approximating the rectus muscles for reconstructing the linea alba. The transversus abdominis is not released, but elements of its release aid in medialization for a straightforward closure.

The surgeon places a 30x30 Bard soft mesh into the large retromuscular pocket, trimming the corners for a better fit. Due to significant oozing, Tisseel is applied, followed by the placement of a retromuscular drain.

So there you have it. Uncomplicated post-op course. Patient stayed overnight and was discharged. I believe that the Madrid modification to TAR allows for a really nice strategy for tension-free closure in settings where you may have some tension on that posterior sheath. Thank you very much. I hope that was educational.
